title: Second Intifada

infobox.value: * [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority")   + [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine") [PLO](/wiki/Palestine_Liberation_Organization "Palestine Liberation Organization")     - [Fatah](/wiki/Fatah "Fatah")     - [Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine](/wiki/Popular_Front_for_the_Liberation_of_Palestine "Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine")     - [Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine](/wiki/Democratic_Front_for_the_Liberation_of_Palestine "Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine")   + [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas")   + [Palestinian Islamic Jihad](/wiki/Palestinian_Islamic_Jihad "Palestinian Islamic Jihad")   + [Popular Resistance Committees](/wiki/Popular_Resistance_Committees "Popular Resistance Committees")

* Palestinian child [Faris Odeh](/wiki/Faris_Odeh "Faris Odeh") throws a stone at an Israeli tank in the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") * Israeli soldiers in [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus") during [Operation Defensive Shield](/wiki/Operation_Defensive_Shield "Operation Defensive Shield") * Aftermath of a [Palestinian suicide bombing](/wiki/Yagur_Junction_bombing "Yagur Junction bombing") on a public transit bus near [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa") * Palestinian protesters confront Israeli security forces near [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah") | |

~1,010[[7]](#cite_note-casualties2-7)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[8]](#cite_note-statspage-8) Israelis total: • 644–773 Israeli civilians killed by Palestinians; • 215–301 Israeli troops killed by Palestinians | **29 September 2000 – 1 January 2005:** 3,179[[8]](#cite_note-statspage-8)[[9]](#cite_note-BBCstats-9)[[10]](#cite_note-un2009jan9-10)–3,354[[7]](#cite_note-casualties2-7) Palestinians total: • 2,739–3,168 Palestinians killed by Israeli troops;**\*** • 152–406 Palestinians killed by Palestinians; • 34 Palestinians killed by Israeli civilians |

* [Suicide attacks](/wiki/List_of_Palestinian_suicide_attacks "List of Palestinian suicide attacks") * [Rocket attacks](/wiki/Palestinian_rocket_attacks_on_Israel "Palestinian rocket attacks on Israel") | |

([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): الانتفاضة الثانية, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *al-Intifāḍa aṯ-Ṯāniya*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'The Second Uprising'; [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): האינתיפאדה השנייה, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *ha-Intifada ha-Shniya*), also known as the **Al-Aqsa Intifada**,[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC2004-11) was a major uprising by [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") against [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and its [occupation](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories") from 2000. Initially sparked by civilian protests in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and areas within Israel proper, the uprising escalated into a prolonged period of heightened violence in Israel, the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") and the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"). This violence, which included shootings, suicide bombings, and military operations, continued until the [Sharm el-Sheikh Summit of 2005](/wiki/Sharm_El_Sheikh_Summit_of_2005 "Sharm El Sheikh Summit of 2005"), which ended hostilities.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC2004-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The **Second Intifada** ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): الانتفاضة الثانية, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *al-Intifāḍa aṯ-Ṯāniya*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'The Second Uprising'; [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): האינתיפאדה השנייה, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *ha-Intifada ha-Shniya*), also known as the **Al-Aqsa Intifada**,[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC2004-11) was a major uprising by [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") against [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and its [occupation](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories") from 2000. Initially sparked by civilian protests in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and areas within Israel proper, the uprising escalated into a prolonged period of heightened violence in Israel, the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") and the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"). This violence, which included shootings, suicide bombings, and military operations, continued until the [Sharm el-Sheikh Summit of 2005](/wiki/Sharm_El_Sheikh_Summit_of_2005 "Sharm El Sheikh Summit of 2005"), which ended hostilities.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC2004-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The general triggers for the unrest are speculated to have been centered on the failure of the [2000 Camp David Summit](/wiki/2000_Camp_David_Summit "2000 Camp David Summit"), which was expected to reach a final agreement on the [Israeli–Palestinian peace process](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_peace_process "Israeli–Palestinian peace process") in July 2000.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPressman2006114-14) An uptick in violent incidents started in September 2000, after Israeli politician [Ariel Sharon](/wiki/Ariel_Sharon "Ariel Sharon") made a provocative visit to the [Temple Mount](/wiki/Temple_Mount "Temple Mount");[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''NPR''2014-15)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPressman2006114-14) the visit itself was peaceful, but, as anticipated, sparked protests and riots that Israeli police put down with rubber bullets, live ammunition, and tear gas.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEByman2011114-16) Israeli security responded with extreme violence, killing over 100 Palestinian protesters within the first few weeks.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Within the first few days of the uprising, the [Israeli military](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") fired one million rounds of ammunition.[[17]](#cite_note-Finkelstein,_2008-17)

A prominent feature of the Second Intifada was a [series of suicide bombings](/wiki/Palestinian_suicide_attacks "Palestinian suicide attacks") carried out by [Palestinian militant groups](/wiki/Palestinian_political_violence "Palestinian political violence"). After March 2001, approximately 138 suicide bombings were conducted by these factions, primarily targeting Israeli civilians.[[18]](#cite_note-:0-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Israeli security forces engaged in gunfights, [targeted killings](/wiki/Targeted_killing_by_Israel "Targeted killing by Israel"), tank attacks, and airstrikes; Palestinians engaged in gunfights, [stone-throwing](/wiki/Palestinian_stone-throwing "Palestinian stone-throwing"), and [rocket attacks](/wiki/Palestinian_rocket_attacks_on_Israel "Palestinian rocket attacks on Israel").[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) With a combined casualty figure for combatants and civilians, the violence is estimated to have resulted in the deaths of approximately 3,000 Palestinians and 1,000 Israelis, as well as 64 foreign nationals.[[25]](#cite_note-casualties2005-25)

The Second Intifada ended with the Sharm el-Sheikh Summit of 2005,[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker2019958-26) as Palestinian president [Mahmoud Abbas](/wiki/Mahmoud_Abbas "Mahmoud Abbas") and Sharon, by then Israel's prime minister, agreed to take definitive steps to de-escalate the hostilities.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbas2005-27)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharon2005-28) They also reaffirmed their commitment to the "[roadmap for peace](/wiki/Road_map_for_peace "Road map for peace")" that had been proposed by the [Quartet on the Middle East](/wiki/Quartet_on_the_Middle_East "Quartet on the Middle East") in 2003. Additionally, Sharon agreed to release 900 [Palestinian prisoners](/wiki/Palestinians_in_Israeli_custody "Palestinians in Israeli custody")[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReinhart200677-29) and further stated that Israeli troops would withdraw from those parts of the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") that they had re-occupied while fighting Palestinian militants during the uprising.

## Etymology

**Second Intifada** refers to a second Palestinian uprising, following the [first Palestinian uprising](/wiki/First_Intifada "First Intifada"), which occurred between December 1987 and 1993. "[Intifada](/wiki/Intifada "Intifada")" (انتفاضة) translates into English as "uprising". Its root is an Arabic word meaning "the shaking off". It has been used in the meaning of "insurrection" in various Arab countries; the [Egyptian riots of 1977](/wiki/1977_Egyptian_bread_riots "1977 Egyptian bread riots"), for example, were called the "bread intifada".[[30]](#cite_note-30) The term refers to a revolt against the Israeli occupation of the [Palestinian territories](/wiki/Palestinian_territories "Palestinian territories").

**Al-Aqsa Intifada** refers to [Al-Aqsa](/wiki/Al-Aqsa "Al-Aqsa"), the main name for the mosque compound constructed in the 8th century CE atop the [Temple Mount](/wiki/Temple_Mount "Temple Mount") in the [Old City of Jerusalem](/wiki/Old_City_(Jerusalem) "Old City (Jerusalem)"), and also known to [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") as the [Haram al-Sharif](/wiki/Haram_al-Sharif "Haram al-Sharif").

The Intifada is sometimes called the **Oslo War** (מלחמת אוסלו) by some Israelis who consider it to be the result of concessions made by Israel following the [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords"),[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) and **Arafat's War**, after the [late Palestinian leader](/wiki/Yasser_Arafat "Yasser Arafat") whom some blamed for starting it. Others have named what they consider disproportionate response to what was initially a popular uprising by unarmed demonstrators as the reason for the escalation of the Intifada into an all-out war.[[34]](#cite_note-SBA2006-34)

## Background

See also: [Palestinian political violence](/wiki/Palestinian_political_violence "Palestinian political violence") and [Israeli-occupied territories](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories")

### Oslo Accords

Under the [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords"), signed in 1993 and 1995, Israel committed to the phased withdrawal of its forces from parts of the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") and [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), and affirmed the Palestinian right to [self-government](/wiki/Self-government "Self-government") within those areas through the creation of a [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority"). For their part, the [Palestine Liberation Organization](/wiki/Palestine_Liberation_Organization "Palestine Liberation Organization") formally recognised Israel and committed to adopting responsibility for internal security in population centres in the areas evacuated. Palestinian self-rule was to last for a five-year interim period during which a permanent agreement would be negotiated. However, the realities on the ground left both sides deeply disappointed with the Oslo process. Palestinian freedom of movement reportedly worsened from 1993 to 2000.[[35]](#cite_note-causes-35) Israelis and Palestinians have blamed each other for the failure of the Oslo peace process. In the five years immediately following the signing of the Oslo accords, 405 Palestinians and 256 Israelis were killed.

From 1996 Israel made extensive contingency plans and preparations, collectively code-named "Musical Charm", in the eventuality that peace talks might fail. In 1998, after concluding that the 5-year plan stipulated in the Oslo Talks would not be completed, the IDF implemented an Operation Field of Thorns plan to conquer towns in Area C, and some areas of Gaza, and military exercises at regimental level were carried out in April 2000 to that end. Palestinian preparations were defensive, and small-scale, more to reassure the local population than to cope with an eventual attack from Israel. The intensity of these operations led one Brigadier General, Zvi Fogel to wonder whether Israel's military preparations would not turn out to be a self-fulfilling prophecy.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

In 1995, [Shimon Peres](/wiki/Shimon_Peres "Shimon Peres") took the place of [Yitzhak Rabin](/wiki/Yitzhak_Rabin "Yitzhak Rabin"), who had been [assassinated](/wiki/Assassination_of_Yitzhak_Rabin "Assassination of Yitzhak Rabin") by [Yigal Amir](/wiki/Yigal_Amir "Yigal Amir"), a Jewish extremist opposed to the Oslo peace agreement. In the 1996 elections, Israelis elected a right-wing[[37]](#cite_note-Schmemann-37) coalition led by the [Likud](/wiki/Likud "Likud") candidate, [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") who was followed in 1999 by the [Labor Party](/wiki/Labor_Party_(Israel) "Labor Party (Israel)") leader [Ehud Barak](/wiki/Ehud_Barak "Ehud Barak").

### Camp David Summit

From 11 to 25 July 2000, the [Middle East Peace Summit at Camp David](/wiki/2000_Camp_David_Summit "2000 Camp David Summit") was held between the United States [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States_of_America "President of the United States of America") [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), Israeli Prime Minister [Ehud Barak](/wiki/Ehud_Barak "Ehud Barak"), and [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority") Chairman [Yasser Arafat](/wiki/Yasser_Arafat "Yasser Arafat"). The talks ultimately failed with each side blaming the other. There were five principal obstacles to agreement: borders and territorial contiguity, [Jerusalem](/wiki/Positions_on_Jerusalem "Positions on Jerusalem") and the [Temple Mount](/wiki/Temple_Mount "Temple Mount"), Palestinian refugees and their [right of return](/wiki/Palestinian_right_of_return "Palestinian right of return"), Israeli security concerns and Israeli settlements. Disappointment at the situation over the summer led to a significant fracturing of the PLO as many Fatah factions abandoned it to join Hamas and Islamic Jihad.[[38]](#cite_note-Rosen-38)

On 13 September 2000, Yasser Arafat and the [Palestinian Legislative Council](/wiki/Palestinian_Legislative_Council "Palestinian Legislative Council") postponed the planned unilateral declaration of an independent Palestinian state.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Israeli settlements

While Peres had limited settlement construction at the request of US Secretary of State, [Madeleine Albright](/wiki/Madeleine_Albright "Madeleine Albright"),[[37]](#cite_note-Schmemann-37) Netanyahu continued construction within existing Israeli settlements[[40]](#cite_note-FMEP-40) and put forward plans for the construction of a new neighbourhood, [Har Homa](/wiki/Har_Homa "Har Homa"), in [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem"). However, he fell far short of the Shamir government's 1991–92 level and refrained from building new settlements, although the Oslo agreements stipulated no such ban.[[37]](#cite_note-Schmemann-37) Construction of housing units before Oslo, 1991–92: 13,960; after Oslo, 1994–95: 3,840; 1996–1997: 3,570.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

To marginalise the settlers' more militant wing, Barak courted moderate settler opinion, securing agreement for the dismantlement of 12 new outposts that had been constructed since the [Wye River Agreement](/wiki/Wye_River_Agreement "Wye River Agreement") of November 1998,[[42]](#cite_note-Youngs-42) but the continued expansion of existing settlements with plans for 3,000 new houses in the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") drew strong condemnation from the Palestinian leadership. Though construction within existing settlements was permitted under the Oslo agreements, Palestinian supporters contend that any continued construction was contrary to its spirit,[[37]](#cite_note-Schmemann-37) prejudiced the outcome of final status negotiations, and undermined Palestinian confidence in Barak's desire for peace.[[42]](#cite_note-Youngs-42)

### 2000

The [Middle East Peace Summit at Camp David](/wiki/2000_Camp_David_Summit "2000 Camp David Summit"), from 11 to 25 July 2000, took place between the United States [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States_of_America "President of the United States of America") [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), Israeli Prime Minister [Ehud Barak](/wiki/Ehud_Barak "Ehud Barak"), and [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority") Chairman [Yasser Arafat](/wiki/Yasser_Arafat "Yasser Arafat"). It failed with the latter two blaming each other for the failure of the talks.[[43]](#cite_note-43) There were four principal obstacles to agreement: territory, [Jerusalem](/wiki/Positions_on_Jerusalem "Positions on Jerusalem") and the [Temple Mount](/wiki/Temple_Mount "Temple Mount"), Palestinian refugees and the [right of return](/wiki/Palestinian_right_of_return "Palestinian right of return"), and Israeli security concerns.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

#### Ariel Sharon visits the Temple Mount

On 28 September, Israeli opposition leader [Ariel Sharon](/wiki/Ariel_Sharon "Ariel Sharon") and a [Likud](/wiki/Likud "Likud") party delegation guarded by hundreds of Israeli riot police visited the [Temple Mount](/wiki/Temple_Mount "Temple Mount"), which is widely considered the [third holiest site in Islam](/wiki/Holiest_sites_in_Islam_(Sunni) "Holiest sites in Islam (Sunni)").[[45]](#cite_note-nytimes_outbreak-45) Israel has claimed sovereignty over the Mount and the rest of East Jerusalem [since 1980](/wiki/Jerusalem_Law "Jerusalem Law"), and the compound is the [holiest site in Judaism](/wiki/Jerusalem_in_Judaism "Jerusalem in Judaism").

The Israeli Interior Minister [Shlomo Ben-Ami](/wiki/Shlomo_Ben-Ami "Shlomo Ben-Ami"), who permitted Sharon's visit, later claimed that he had telephoned the Palestinian Authority's security chief [Jibril Rajoub](/wiki/Jibril_Rajoub "Jibril Rajoub") before the visit and gotten his reassurances that as long as Sharon didn't enter the mosques his visit wouldn't cause any problems. Rajoub vociferously denied having given any such reassurances.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregman2005160-46)

Shortly after Sharon left the site, angry demonstrations by Palestinian Jerusalemites outside erupted into rioting. The person in charge of the [waqf](/wiki/Waqf "Waqf") at the time, Abu Qteish, was later indicted by Israel for using a loud-speaker to call on Palestinians to defend Al-Aqsa, which action Israeli authorities claimed was responsible for the subsequent stone-throwing in the direction of the Wailing Wall.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Israeli police responded with tear gas and rubber bullets, while protesters [hurled stones](/wiki/Palestinian_stone-throwing "Palestinian stone-throwing") and other projectiles, injuring 25 policemen, of whom one was seriously injured and had to be taken to hospital. At least three Palestinians were wounded by rubber bullets.[[48]](#cite_note-bbc2000sept28-48)

The stated purpose for Sharon's visit of the compound was to assert the right of all Israelis to visit the Temple Mount;[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) however, according to Likud spokesman [Ofir Akunis](/wiki/Ofir_Akunis "Ofir Akunis"), the actual purpose was to "show that under a Likud government [the Temple Mount] will remain under Israeli sovereignty."[[51]](#cite_note-51) Ehud Barak in the Camp David negotiations had insisted that East Jerusalem, where the Haram was located, would remain under complete Israeli sovereignty.[[52]](#cite_note-Singh-52) In response to accusations by Ariel Sharon of government readiness to concede the site to the Palestinians, the Israeli government gave Sharon permission to visit the area. When alerted of his intentions, senior Palestinian figures, such as [Yasser Arafat](/wiki/Yasser_Arafat "Yasser Arafat"), [Saeb Erekat](/wiki/Saeb_Erekat "Saeb Erekat"), and [Faisal Husseini](/wiki/Faisal_Husseini "Faisal Husseini"), all asked Sharon to call off his visit.[[53]](#cite_note-Jerpro-53)

Ten days earlier the Palestinians had observed their annual memorial day for the [Sabra and Shatila massacre](/wiki/Sabra_and_Shatila_massacre "Sabra and Shatila massacre"), where thousands of [Lebanese](/wiki/Islam_in_Lebanon "Islam in Lebanon") and [Palestinian Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Palestine "Islam in Palestine") were massacred by [Lebanese Forces](/wiki/Lebanese_Forces_(militia) "Lebanese Forces (militia)") supported by the Israeli military.[[53]](#cite_note-Jerpro-53) The Israeli [Kahan Commission](/wiki/Kahan_Commission "Kahan Commission") had concluded that [Ariel Sharon](/wiki/Ariel_Sharon "Ariel Sharon"), who was the Israeli Defense Minister during the Sabra and Shatila massacre, was found to bear personal responsibility[[54]](#cite_note-54) "for ignoring the danger of bloodshed and revenge" and "not taking appropriate measures to prevent bloodshed." Sharon's negligence in protecting the civilian population of Beirut, which had come under Israeli control, amounted to a *non-fulfillment of a duty with which the Defence Minister was charged*, and it was recommended that Sharon be dismissed as Defence Minister. Sharon initially refused to resign, but after the death of an Israeli after a peace march, Sharon did resign as Defense minister, but remained in the Israeli cabinet.

The Palestinians condemned Sharon's visit to the Temple Mount as a provocation and an incursion, as were his armed bodyguards that arrived on the scene with him. Critics claim that Sharon knew that the visit could trigger violence, and that the purpose of his visit was political. According to one observer, Sharon, in walking on the Temple Mount, was "skating on the thinnest ice in the Arab-Israeli conflict."[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShindler2013283-55)

According to *The New York Times*, many in the Arab world, including Egyptians, Palestinians, Lebanese and Jordanians, point to Sharon's visit as the beginning of the Second Intifada and derailment of the peace process.[[56]](#cite_note-56) According to Juliana Ochs, Sharon's visit 'symbolically instigated' the second intifada.[[57]](#cite_note-57) [Marwan Barghouti](/wiki/Marwan_Barghouti "Marwan Barghouti") said that although Sharon's provocative actions were a rallying point for Palestinians, the Second Intifada would have erupted even had he not visited the Temple Mount.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

#### Post-visit Palestinian riots

On 29 September 2000, the day after Sharon's visit, following Friday prayers, large riots broke out around the [Old City of Jerusalem](/wiki/Old_City_of_Jerusalem "Old City of Jerusalem"). Israeli police fired at Palestinians at the Temple Mount throwing stones over the [Western Wall](/wiki/Western_Wall "Western Wall") at Jewish worshippers. After the chief of Jerusalem's police force was knocked unconscious by a stone, they switched to live ammunition and killed four Palestinian youths.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShindler2013283-55)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) Up to 200 Palestinians and police were injured.[[61]](#cite_note-cnn20000929-61) Another three Palestinians were killed in the Old City and on the [Mount of Olives](/wiki/Mount_of_Olives "Mount of Olives").[[53]](#cite_note-Jerpro-53) By the end of the day, seven Palestinians had been killed and 300 had been wounded;[[53]](#cite_note-Jerpro-53) 70 Israeli policemen were also injured in the clashes.[[53]](#cite_note-Jerpro-53)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

In the days that followed, demonstrations erupted all over the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") and [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"). Israeli police responded with live fire and rubber-coated bullets. In the first five days, at least 47 Palestinians were killed, and 1,885 were wounded.[[53]](#cite_note-Jerpro-53) In Paris, as [Jacques Chirac](/wiki/Jacques_Chirac "Jacques Chirac") attempted to mediate between the parties, he protested to Barak that the ratio of Palestinians and Israelis killed and wounded on one day were such that he could not convince anyone the Palestinians were the aggressors. He also told Barak that "continu(ing) to fire from helicopters on people throwing rocks" and refusing an international inquiry was tantamount to rejecting Arafat's offer to participate in trilateral negotiations.[[63]](#cite_note-63) During the first few days of riots, the IDF fired approximately 1.3 million bullets.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

According to [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") the early Palestinian casualties were those taking part in demonstrations or bystanders. Amnesty further states that approximately 80% of the Palestinians killed during the first month were in demonstrations where Israeli security services lives were not in danger.[[65]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International-65)

On 30 September 2000, the death of [Muhammad al-Durrah](/wiki/Muhammad_al-Durrah_incident "Muhammad al-Durrah incident"), a Palestinian boy shot dead while sheltering behind his father in an alley in the Gaza Strip, was caught on video. Initially the boy's death and his father's wounding was attributed to Israeli soldiers. The scene assumed iconic status, as it was shown around the world and repeatedly broadcast on Arab television. The Israeli army initially assumed responsibility for the killing and apologised, and only retracted 2 months later, when an internal investigation cast doubt on the original version, and controversy subsequently raged as to whether indeed the IDF had fired the shots or Palestinian factions were responsible for the fatal gunshots.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

#### October 2000 events

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Andartnazareth.jpg/250px-Andartnazareth.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andartnazareth.jpg)

Monument to Israeli Arab casualties in October 2000 riots, Nazareth

Main article: [October 2000 events](/wiki/October_2000_events "October 2000 events")

The "October 2000 events" refers to several days of disturbances and clashes within Israel, mostly between [Arab citizens](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel "Arab citizens of Israel") and the [Israel police](/wiki/Israel_police "Israel police"), as well as large-scale rioting by both Arabs and Jews. Twelve Arab citizens of Israel and a Palestinian from the Gaza Strip were killed by Israeli police, while an Israeli Jew was killed when his car was hit by a rock on the [Tel-Aviv-Haifa freeway](/wiki/Highway_2_(Israel) "Highway 2 (Israel)"). During the first month of the Intifada, 141 Palestinians were killed and 5,984 were wounded, while 12 Israelis were killed and 65 wounded.[[67]](#cite_note-Catignani-67)

A general strike and demonstrations across northern Israel began on 1 October and continued for several days. In some cases, the demonstrations escalated into clashes with the [Israeli police](/wiki/Israel_police "Israel police") involving [rock-throwing](/wiki/Palestinian_stone_throwing "Palestinian stone throwing"), [firebombing](/wiki/Molotov_cocktail "Molotov cocktail"), and live-fire. Policemen used tear-gas and opened fire with [rubber-coated bullets](/wiki/Rubber_bullet "Rubber bullet") and later live ammunition in some instances, many times in contravention of police protocol governing riot-dispersion. This use of live ammunition was directly linked with many of the deaths by the [Or Commission](/wiki/Or_Commission "Or Commission").

On 8 October, thousands of Jewish Israelis participated in violent acts in Tel Aviv and elsewhere, some throwing stones at Arabs, destroying Arab property and chanting "Death to the Arabs."[[68]](#cite_note-68)

Following the riots, a high degree of tension between Jewish and Arab citizens and distrust between the Arab citizens and police were widespread. An investigation committee, headed by Supreme Court Justice [Theodor Or](/w/index.php?title=Theodor_Or&action=edit&redlink=1 "Theodor Or (page does not exist)"), reviewed the violent riots and found that the police were poorly prepared to handle such riots and charged major officers with bad conduct. The [Or Commission](/wiki/Or_Commission "Or Commission") reprimanded Prime Minister [Ehud Barak](/wiki/Ehud_Barak "Ehud Barak") and recommended [Shlomo Ben-Ami](/wiki/Shlomo_Ben-Ami "Shlomo Ben-Ami"), then the Internal Security Minister, not serve again as Minister of Public Security. The committee also blamed Arab leaders and Knesset members for contributing to inflaming the atmosphere and making the violence more severe.

#### Ramallah lynching and Israeli response

Main article: [2000 Ramallah lynching](/wiki/2000_Ramallah_lynching "2000 Ramallah lynching")

On 12 October, PA police arrested two Israeli reservists who had accidentally entered [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah"), where in the preceding weeks a hundred Palestinians had been killed, nearly two dozen of them minors.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Rumours quickly spread that Israeli undercover agents were in the building, and an angry crowd of more than 1,000 Palestinians gathered in front of the station calling for their death. Both soldiers were beaten, stabbed, and disembowelled, and one body was set on fire. An Italian television crew captured the killings on video and then broadcast the tape internationally.[[70]](#cite_note-BBClynch-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) A British journalist had his camera destroyed by rioters as he attempted to take a picture. The brutality of the killings shocked the Israeli public, who saw it as proof of a deep-seated Palestinian hatred of Israel and Jews.[[72]](#cite_note-72) In response, Israel launched a series of retaliatory air-strikes against Palestinian Authority targets in the West Bank and Gaza Strip. The police station where the lynching had taken place was evacuated and destroyed in these operations.[[73]](#cite_note-revenge-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) Israel later tracked down and arrested those responsible for killing the soldiers.

#### November–December 2000

Clashes between Israeli forces and Palestinians increased sharply on 1 November, when three Israeli soldiers and six Palestinians were killed, and four IDF soldiers and 140 Palestinians were wounded. In subsequent days, casualties increased as the IDF attempted to restore order, with clashes occurring every day in November. A total of 122 Palestinians and 22 Israelis were killed. On 27 November, the first day of [Ramadan](/wiki/Ramadan "Ramadan"), Israel eased restrictions on the passage of goods and fuel through the [Karni crossing](/wiki/Karni_crossing "Karni crossing"). That same day, the Jerusalem settlement of [Gilo](/wiki/Gilo "Gilo") came under Palestinian heavy machine gun fire from [Beit Jala](/wiki/Beit_Jala "Beit Jala"). Israel tightened restrictions a week later, and Palestinians continued to clash with the IDF and Israeli settlers, with a total of 51 Palestinians and 8 Israelis killed in December.[[75]](#cite_note-75) In a last attempt by the Clinton administration to achieve a peace deal between Israelis and Palestinians, a summit was planned in Sharm el-Sheikh in December. However, Israeli Prime Minister Barak decided not to attend after the Palestinians delayed their acceptance of the [Clinton Parameters](/wiki/Clinton_Parameters "Clinton Parameters").[[76]](#cite_note-76)

### 2001

The [Taba Summit](/wiki/Taba_Summit "Taba Summit") between Israel and the [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority") was held from 21 to 27 January 2001, at [Taba](/wiki/Taba_(Egypt) "Taba (Egypt)") in the [Sinai peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_peninsula "Sinai peninsula"). Israeli prime minister [Ehud Barak](/wiki/Ehud_Barak "Ehud Barak") and Palestinian President [Yasser Arafat](/wiki/Yasser_Arafat "Yasser Arafat") came closer to reaching a final settlement than any previous or subsequent peace talks yet ultimately failed to achieve their goals.

On 17 January 2001, Israeli teenager [Ofir Rahum](/wiki/Ofir_Rahum "Ofir Rahum") was murdered after being lured into [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah") by a 24-year-old Palestinian, Mona Jaud Awana, a member of Fatah's [Tanzim](/wiki/Tanzim "Tanzim"). She had contacted Ofir on the internet and engaged in an online romance with him for several months. She eventually convinced him to drive to Ramallah to meet her, where he was instead ambushed by three Palestinian gunmen and shot over fifteen times.[[77]](#cite_note-Wired-77) Awana was later arrested in a massive military and police operation, and imprisoned for life. Five other Israelis were killed in January, along with eighteen Palestinians.

[Ariel Sharon](/wiki/Ariel_Sharon "Ariel Sharon"), at the time from the [Likud](/wiki/Likud "Likud") party, ran against [Ehud Barak](/wiki/Ehud_Barak "Ehud Barak") from the [Labor party](/wiki/Labor_(Israel) "Labor (Israel)"). Sharon was elected Israeli Prime Minister 6 February 2001 in the [2001 special election to the Prime Ministership](/wiki/2001_Israeli_prime_ministerial_election "2001 Israeli prime ministerial election"). Sharon refused to meet in person with Yasser Arafat.

Violence in March resulted in the deaths of 8 Israelis, mostly civilians, and 26 Palestinians. In [Hebron](/wiki/Hebron "Hebron"), a Palestinian sniper killed ten-month-old Israeli baby [Shalhevet Pass](/wiki/Shalhevet_Pass "Shalhevet Pass").[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) The murder shocked the Israeli public. According to the Israel police investigation the sniper aimed deliberately at the baby.[[80]](#cite_note-education-80)

On 30 April 2001, seven Palestinian militants were killed in an explosion, one of them a participant in Ofir Rahum's murder. The IDF refused to confirm or deny Palestinian accusations that it was responsible.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Residents_run_away_during_a_home_demolition_in_Gaza_during_the_second_intifada.jpg/250px-Residents_run_away_during_a_home_demolition_in_Gaza_during_the_second_intifada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Residents_run_away_during_a_home_demolition_in_Gaza_during_the_second_intifada.jpg)

[Israeli armoured bulldozer](/wiki/IDF_Caterpillar_D9 "IDF Caterpillar D9") demolishing a Palestinian house in Gaza city, 2001

On 7 May 2001, IDF [naval commandos](/wiki/Shayetet_13 "Shayetet 13") captured the vessel *[Santorini](/wiki/Santorini_affair "Santorini affair")*, which was sailing in international waters towards Palestinian Authority-controlled Gaza. The ship was laden with weaponry. The Israeli investigation that followed said that the shipment had been purchased by [Ahmed Jibril](/wiki/Ahmed_Jibril "Ahmed Jibril")'s [Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine - General Command](/wiki/Popular_Front_for_the_Liberation_of_Palestine_-_General_Command "Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine - General Command") (PFLP-GC). The ship's value and that of its cargo was estimated at $10 million. The crew was reportedly planning to unload the cargo of weapons-filled barrels—carefully sealed and waterproofed along with their contents—at a prearranged location off the Gaza coast, where the Palestinian Authority would recover it.

On 8 May 2001, two Israeli teenagers, Yaakov "Koby" Mandell (13) and Yosef Ishran (14), were kidnapped while hiking near their village. Their bodies were discovered the next morning in a cave near where they lived.[[81]](#cite_note-Guardian-81) *USA Today* reported that, according to the police, both boys had "been bound, stabbed and beaten to death with rocks." The newspaper continued, "The walls of the cave in the Judean Desert were covered with the boys' blood, reportedly smeared there by the killers."[[82]](#cite_note-USA-82)

After a [suicide bombing](/wiki/Suicide_bombing "Suicide bombing") [struck Netanya](/wiki/2001_HaSharon_Mall_suicide_bombing "2001 HaSharon Mall suicide bombing") on 18 May 2001, Israel for the first time since 1967 used warplanes to attack Palestinian Authority targets in the West Bank and Gaza, killing 12 Palestinians. In the past, airstrikes had been carried out with helicopter gunships.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Dolphinarium_Massacre_memorial_in_Tel_Aviv.JPG/250px-Dolphinarium_Massacre_memorial_in_Tel_Aviv.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dolphinarium_Massacre_memorial_in_Tel_Aviv.JPG)

Dolphinarium Massacre memorial at the Tel Aviv Dolphinarium site with the names of the victims written in Russian

On 1 June 2001, an [Islamic Jihad](/wiki/Islamic_Jihad_Movement_in_Palestine "Islamic Jihad Movement in Palestine") suicide bomber [detonated himself in the Tel Aviv coastline Dolphinarium](/wiki/Dolphinarium_massacre "Dolphinarium massacre") dancing club. Twenty-one Israeli civilians, most of them high school students, were killed and 132 injured.[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87) The attack significantly hampered American attempts to negotiate a cease-fire.

The 12 June [Murder of Georgios Tsibouktzakis](/wiki/Murder_of_Georgios_Tsibouktzakis "Murder of Georgios Tsibouktzakis") by Palestinian snipers was later tied to [Marwan Barghouti](/wiki/Marwan_Barghouti "Marwan Barghouti").[[88]](#cite_note-EnevBarghouti-88)

A total of 469 Palestinians and 199 Israelis were killed in 2001. Amnesty International's report on the first year of the Intifada states:

> The overwhelming majority of cases of unlawful killings and injuries in Israel and the Occupied Territories have been committed by the IDF using excessive force. In particular, the IDF have used US-supplied helicopters in punitive rocket attacks where there was no imminent danger to life. Israel has also used helicopter gunships to carry out extrajudicial executions and to fire at targets that resulted in the killing of civilians, including children. ... Hamas and Islamic Jihad have frequently placed bombs in public places, usually within Israel, in order to kill and maim large numbers of Israeli civilians in a random manner. Both organizations have fostered a cult of martyrdom and frequently use suicide bombers.[[65]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International-65)

Palestinian terrorists committed a number of suicide attacks later in 2001, among them the [Sbarro restaurant massacre](/wiki/Sbarro_restaurant_massacre "Sbarro restaurant massacre"), with 15 civilian casualties (including 7 children);[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90) the [Nahariya train station suicide bombing](/wiki/Nahariya_train_station_suicide_bombing "Nahariya train station suicide bombing") and the [Pardes Hanna bus bombing](/wiki/Pardes_Hanna_bus_bombing "Pardes Hanna bus bombing"), both with 3 civilian casualties;[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-Haaretz-92)[[93]](#cite_note-Blade-93) the Ben Yehuda Street bombing with 11 civilian deaths, many of them children;[[94]](#cite_note-94) and the [Haifa bus 16 suicide bombing](/wiki/Haifa_bus_16_suicide_bombing "Haifa bus 16 suicide bombing"), with 15 civilian casualties.[[95]](#cite_note-B'Tselem-95)

### 2002

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Karin_A_weapons.jpg/250px-Karin_A_weapons.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karin_A_weapons.jpg)

Military equipment confiscated from [Karine A](/wiki/Karine_A "Karine A")

In January 2002, the [IDF](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") [Shayetet 13](/wiki/Shayetet_13 "Shayetet 13") naval commandos captured the *[Karine A](/wiki/Karine_A "Karine A")*, a freighter carrying weapons from [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") towards Israel, believed to be intended for Palestinian militant use against Israel. It was discovered that top officials in the [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority") were involved in the smuggling, with the Israelis pointing the finger towards [Yasser Arafat](/wiki/Yasser_Arafat "Yasser Arafat") as also being involved.

Palestinians launched a spate of suicide bombings and attacks against Israel, aimed mostly at civilians. On 3 March, a Palestinian sniper killed 10 Israeli soldiers and settlers and wounded 4 at a checkpoint near [Ofra](/wiki/Ofra "Ofra"),[[96]](#cite_note-96) using an [M1 Carbine](/wiki/M1_Carbine "M1 Carbine"). He was later arrested and sentenced to life imprisonment. The rate of the attacks increased, and was at its highest in March 2002.[[97]](#cite_note-Fal09-97)

In addition to numerous shooting and grenade attacks, the month saw 15 suicide bombings carried out in Israel — an average of one bombing every two days. The high rate of attacks caused widespread fear throughout Israel and serious disruption of daily life throughout the country. March 2002 became known in Israel as "Black March".[[97]](#cite_note-Fal09-97) On 12 March [United Nations Security Council Resolution 1397](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_1397 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 1397") was passed, which reaffirmed a [Two-state solution](/wiki/Two-state_solution "Two-state solution") and laid the groundwork for a [Road map for peace](/wiki/Road_map_for_peace "Road map for peace").[[98]](#cite_note-Rub03-98)

On 27 March, the wave of violence culminated with a suicide bombing during a [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") celebration at the Park Hotel in [Netanya](/wiki/Netanya "Netanya") in which 30 people were killed. The attack became known as the [Passover massacre](/wiki/Passover_massacre "Passover massacre").[[99]](#cite_note-Bts_ODS-99) In total, around 130 Israelis, mostly civilians, were killed in Palestinian attacks during March 2002.[[98]](#cite_note-Rub03-98) On 28 March, Arab leaders, whose constituencies were exposed to detailed television coverage of the violence in the conflict, set out a comprehensive [Arab Peace Initiative](/wiki/Arab_Peace_Initiative "Arab Peace Initiative") that was endorsed by Arafat, but virtually ignored by Israel.[[98]](#cite_note-Rub03-98)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattar200540-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102)

On 29 March, Israel launched [Operation Defensive Shield](/wiki/Operation_Defensive_Shield "Operation Defensive Shield"), which lasted until 3 May. The IDF made sweeping incursions throughout the West Bank, and into numerous Palestinian cities. Arafat was put under siege in his [Ramallah compound](/wiki/Mukataa "Mukataa").[[103]](#cite_note-103) The UN estimated that 497 Palestinians were killed and 1,447 wounded by the Israeli incursion from 1 March to 7 May.[[104]](#cite_note-UNPressRelease-104) A UN report cleared Israel of allegations of massacre, but criticized it for using excessive force on the civilian population. Israeli forces also arrested 4,258 Palestinians during the operation.[[105]](#cite_note-105) Israeli casualties during the operation totaled 30 dead and 127 wounded. The operation culminated with the recapturing of Palestinian Authority controlled areas.[[104]](#cite_note-UNPressRelease-104)

#### Battle of Jenin

Main article: [Battle of Jenin (2002)](/wiki/Battle_of_Jenin_(2002) "Battle of Jenin (2002)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/IDF-D9L003.jpg/250px-IDF-D9L003.jpg)](/wiki/File:IDF-D9L003.jpg)

[IDF Caterpillar D9](/wiki/IDF_Caterpillar_D9 "IDF Caterpillar D9") armored bulldozer

Between 2 and 11 April, a siege and fierce fighting took place in the Palestinian refugee camp of the city of [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin"). The camp was targeted during Operation Defensive Shield after Israel determined that it had "served as a launch site for numerous terrorist attacks against both Israeli civilians and Israeli towns and villages in the area."[[106]](#cite_note-mfa1-106) The Jenin battle became a flashpoint for both sides, and saw fierce urban combat as Israeli infantry supported by armor and attack helicopters fought to clear the camp of Palestinian militants. The battle was eventually won by the IDF, after it employed a dozen [Caterpillar D9](/wiki/IDF_Caterpillar_D9 "IDF Caterpillar D9") [armored bulldozers](/wiki/Armored_bulldozer "Armored bulldozer") to clear Palestinian [booby traps](/wiki/Booby_trap "Booby trap"), detonate explosive charges, and raze buildings and gun-posts; the bulldozers proved impervious to attacks by Palestinian militants.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/%D7%92%27%D7%A0%D7%99%D7%9F_%D7%97%D7%95%D7%9E%D7%AA_%D7%9E%D7%92%D7%9F_1.jpg/250px-%D7%92%27%D7%A0%D7%99%D7%9F_%D7%97%D7%95%D7%9E%D7%AA_%D7%9E%D7%92%D7%9F_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D7%92%27%D7%A0%D7%99%D7%9F_%D7%97%D7%95%D7%9E%D7%AA_%D7%9E%D7%92%D7%9F_1.jpg)

Israeli tanks in the streets of the Palestinian refugee camp of Jenin, April 2002.

During Israeli military operations in the camp, Palestinian sources alleged that a massacre of hundreds of people had taken place. A senior Palestinian Authority official said in mid-April that some 500 had been killed.[[108]](#cite_note-108) During the fighting in Jenin, Israeli officials had also initially estimated hundreds of Palestinian deaths, but later said they expected the Palestinian toll to reach "45 to 55."[[109]](#cite_note-NYT_UN-109) In the ensuing controversy, Israel blocked the United Nations from conducting the first-hand inquiry unanimously sought by the Security Council, but the UN nonetheless felt able to dismiss claims of a massacre in its report, which said there had been approximately 52 deaths, criticising both sides for placing Palestinian civilians at risk.[[109]](#cite_note-NYT_UN-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110) Based on their own investigations, [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International")[[111]](#cite_note-111) and [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch")[[112]](#cite_note-HRW_May-112) charged that some IDF personnel in Jenin had committed [war crimes](/wiki/War_crimes "War crimes") but also confirmed that no massacre had been committed by the IDF. Both human rights organizations called for official inquiries; the IDF disputed the charges.

After the battle, most sources, including the IDF and [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority"), placed the Palestinian death toll at 52–56;[[113]](#cite_note-martinWT-113) [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") documented 52 Palestinian deaths and claimed that it included at least 27 militants and 22 civilians, and an additional 3 Palestinians whose status as militants or civilians could not be ascertained,[[114]](#cite_note-hrwreport-114) while the IDF said that 48 militants and 5 civilians had been killed.[[115]](#cite_note-harel257-258-115) According to Human Rights Watch, 140 buildings had been destroyed.[[116]](#cite_note-116) The IDF reported that 23 Israeli soldiers had been killed and 75 wounded during the battle.[[112]](#cite_note-HRW_May-112)[[117]](#cite_note-117)

#### Siege in Bethlehem

Main article: [Siege of the Church of the Nativity in Bethlehem](/wiki/Siege_of_the_Church_of_the_Nativity_in_Bethlehem "Siege of the Church of the Nativity in Bethlehem")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/%D7%97%D7%95%D7%9E%D7%AA_%D7%9E%D7%92%D7%9F_37.jpg/250px-%D7%97%D7%95%D7%9E%D7%AA_%D7%9E%D7%92%D7%9F_37.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D7%97%D7%95%D7%9E%D7%AA_%D7%9E%D7%92%D7%9F_37.jpg)

Israeli sniper during the [siege of the Church of the Nativity in Bethlehem](/wiki/Siege_of_the_Church_of_the_Nativity "Siege of the Church of the Nativity"), April 3, 2002

From 2 April to 10 May, a stand-off developed at the [Church of the Nativity](/wiki/Church_of_the_Nativity "Church of the Nativity") in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"). IDF soldiers surrounded the church while Palestinian civilians, militants, and priests were inside. During the [siege](/wiki/Siege_of_the_Church_of_the_Nativity "Siege of the Church of the Nativity"), IDF snipers killed 8 militants inside the church and wounded more than 40 people. The stand-off was resolved by the deportation to Europe of 13 Palestinian militants whom the IDF had identified as terrorists, and the IDF ended its 38-day stand-off with the militants inside the church.

### 2003

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Autobus_v_Haif%C4%9B.jpg/250px-Autobus_v_Haif%C4%9B.jpg)](/wiki/File:Autobus_v_Haif%C4%9B.jpg)

The aftermath of a bus bombing in Haifa in 2003

Following an Israeli intelligence report stating that [Yasir Arafat](/wiki/Yasir_Arafat "Yasir Arafat") had paid $20,000 to [al-Aqsa Martyrs' Brigades](/wiki/Al-Aqsa_Martyrs%27_Brigades "Al-Aqsa Martyrs' Brigades"), the United States demanded democratic reforms in the [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority"), as well the appointment of a prime minister independent of Arafat. On 13 March 2003, following U.S. pressure, Arafat appointed [Mahmoud Abbas](/wiki/Mahmoud_Abbas "Mahmoud Abbas") as Palestinian prime minister.

Following the appointment of Abbas, the U.S. administration promoted the [Road map for peace](/wiki/Road_map_for_peace "Road map for peace")—the [Quartet](/wiki/Quartet_on_the_Middle_East "Quartet on the Middle East")'s plan to end the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict") by disbanding militant organizations, halting settlement activity and establishing a democratic and peaceful Palestinian state. The first phase of the plan demanded that the Palestinian Authority suppress guerrilla and terrorist attacks and confiscate illegal weapons. Unable or unwilling to confront militant organizations and risk civil war, Abbas tried to reach a temporary cease-fire agreement with the militant factions and asked them to halt attacks on Israeli civilians.

On 20 May, Israeli naval commandos intercepted another vessel, the *[Abu Hassan](/w/index.php?title=Abu_Hasan_(boat)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Abu Hasan (boat) (page does not exist)")*, on course to the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") from [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"). It was loaded with rockets, weapons, and ammunition. Eight crew members on board were arrested including a senior [Hezbollah](/wiki/Hezbollah "Hezbollah") member.

On 29 June 2003, a [temporary armistice](/wiki/Hudna "Hudna") was unilaterally declared by [Fatah](/wiki/Fatah "Fatah"), [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") and [Islamic Jihad](/wiki/Palestinian_Islamic_Jihad_Movement "Palestinian Islamic Jihad Movement"), which declared a ceasefire and halt to all attacks against Israel for a period of three months.[[118]](#cite_note-118) Violence decreased somewhat in the following month, but suicide bombings against Israeli civilians continued as well as Israeli operations against militants.

Four Palestinians, three of them militants, were killed in gun battles during an IDF raid of [Askar](/wiki/Askar_(Palestine) "Askar (Palestine)") near [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus") involving tanks and [armoured personnel carriers](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier") (APCs); an Israeli soldier was killed by one of the militants. Nearby Palestinians claimed a squad of Israeli police disguised as Palestinian labourers opened fire on [Abbedullah Qawasameh](/wiki/Members_of_Hamas_called_Qawasameh "Members of Hamas called Qawasameh") as he left a Hebron mosque.[[119]](#cite_note-119) [YAMAM](/wiki/YAMAM "YAMAM"), the Israeli counter-terrorism police unit that performed the operation, said Qawasemah opened fire on them as they attempted to arrest him.

On 19 August, Hamas coordinated a [suicide attack](/wiki/Suicide_attack "Suicide attack") on a [crowded bus](/wiki/Jerusalem_bus_2_massacre "Jerusalem bus 2 massacre") in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") killing 23 Israeli civilians, including 7 children. Hamas claimed it was a retaliation for the killing of five Palestinians (including Hamas leader [Abbedullah Qawasameh](/wiki/Members_of_Hamas_called_Qawasameh "Members of Hamas called Qawasameh")) earlier in the week. U.S. and Israeli media outlets frequently referred to the bus bombing as shattering the quiet and bringing an end to the ceasefire.

Following the Hamas bus attack, [Israeli Defence Forces](/wiki/Israeli_Defence_Forces "Israeli Defence Forces") were ordered to kill or capture all Hamas leaders in [Hebron](/wiki/Hebron "Hebron") and the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"). The plotters of the bus suicide bombing were all captured or killed and [Hamas leadership in Hebron](/wiki/Members_of_Hamas_called_Qawasameh "Members of Hamas called Qawasameh") was badly damaged by the IDF. Strict curfews were enforced in Nablus, Jenin, and Tulkarem; the Nablus lockdown lasted for over 100 days. In [Nazlet 'Issa](/wiki/Nazlet_%27Issa "Nazlet 'Issa"), over 60 shops were destroyed by Israeli civil administration [bulldozers](/wiki/Bulldozer "Bulldozer"). The Israeli civil administration explained that the shops were [demolished](/wiki/House_demolition "House demolition") because they were built without a permit. Palestinians consider Israeli military curfews and property destruction to constitute [collective punishment](/wiki/Collective_punishment "Collective punishment") against innocent Palestinians.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/IDF-Caterpillar-D9N-1133.jpg/250px-IDF-Caterpillar-D9N-1133.jpg)](/wiki/File:IDF-Caterpillar-D9N-1133.jpg)

Early Israeli construction of [West Bank barrier](/wiki/West_Bank_barrier "West Bank barrier"), 2003

Unable to rule effectively under Arafat, Abbas resigned in September 2003. [Ahmed Qurei](/wiki/Ahmed_Qurei "Ahmed Qurei") (Abu Ala) was appointed to replace him. The Israeli government gave up hope for negotiated settlement to the conflict and pursued a unilateral policy of physically separating Israel from Palestinian communities by beginning construction on the [Israeli West Bank barrier](/wiki/Israeli_West_Bank_barrier "Israeli West Bank barrier"). Israel claims the barrier is necessary to prevent Palestinian attackers from entering Israeli cities. Palestinians claim the barrier separates Palestinian communities from each other and that the construction plan is a de facto annexation of Palestinian territory.

Following a 4 October [suicide bombing in Maxim restaurant](/wiki/Maxim_restaurant_suicide_bombing "Maxim restaurant suicide bombing"), [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa"), which claimed the lives of 21 Israelis, Israel claimed that Syria and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") sponsored the [Islamic Jihad](/wiki/Palestinian_Islamic_Jihad_Movement "Palestinian Islamic Jihad Movement") and [Hezbollah](/wiki/Hezbollah "Hezbollah"), and were responsible for the terrorist attack. The day after the Maxim massacre, [IAF](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") warplanes [bombed an alleged former Palestinian training base](/wiki/Ain_es_Saheb_airstrike "Ain es Saheb airstrike") at Ain Saheb, [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), which had been mostly abandoned since the 1980s. Munitions being stored on the site were destroyed, and a civilian guard was injured.

### 2004

In response to repeated shelling of Israeli communities with [Qassam rockets](/wiki/Qassam_rocket "Qassam rocket") and mortar shells from Gaza, the [IDF](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") operated mainly in [Rafah](/wiki/Rafah "Rafah") – to search and destroy [smuggling tunnels](/wiki/Gaza_Strip_smuggling_tunnels "Gaza Strip smuggling tunnels") used by militants to obtain [weapons](/wiki/Weapon "Weapon"), [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition "Ammunition"), fugitives, cigarettes, car parts, electrical goods, foreign currency, gold, [drugs](/wiki/Recreational_drug_use "Recreational drug use"), and cloth from [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"). Between September 2000 and May 2004, ninety tunnels connecting Egypt and the Gaza Strip were found and destroyed. Raids in Rafah left many families homeless. Israel's official stance is that their houses were captured by militants and were destroyed during battles with IDF forces. Many of these houses are abandoned due to Israeli incursions and later destroyed. According to Human Rights Watch, over 1,500 houses were destroyed to create a large buffer zone in the city, many "in the absence of military necessity", displacing around sixteen thousand people.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

On 2 February 2004, Israeli Prime Minister [Ariel Sharon](/wiki/Ariel_Sharon "Ariel Sharon") announced his plan to transfer all the [Jewish settlers](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") from the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"). The Israeli opposition dismissed his announcement as "media spin", but the [Israeli Labour Party](/wiki/Labor_Party_(Israel) "Labor Party (Israel)") said it would support such a move. Sharon's right-wing coalition partners [National Religious Party](/wiki/National_Religious_Party "National Religious Party") and [National Union](/wiki/National_Union_(Israel) "National Union (Israel)") rejected the plan and vowed to quit the government if it were implemented. [Yossi Beilin](/wiki/Yossi_Beilin "Yossi Beilin"), peace advocate and architect of the [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords") and the [Geneva Accord](/wiki/Geneva_Accord_(2003) "Geneva Accord (2003)"), also rejected the proposed withdrawal plan. He claimed that withdrawing from the Gaza Strip without a peace agreement would reward [terror](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism").

Following the declaration of the [disengagement plan](/wiki/Israel%27s_unilateral_disengagement_plan "Israel's unilateral disengagement plan") by Ariel Sharon and as a response to suicide attacks on [Erez crossing](/wiki/Israeli_Gaza_Strip_barrier "Israeli Gaza Strip barrier") and [Ashdod](/wiki/Ashdod "Ashdod") seaport (10 people were killed), the IDF launched a series of armored raids on the Gaza Strip (mainly Rafah and refugee camps around Gaza), killing about 70 [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") militants. On 22 March 2004, an Israeli helicopter gunship [killed](/wiki/Israeli_targeted_killings "Israeli targeted killings") Hamas leader Sheikh [Ahmed Yassin](/wiki/Ahmed_Yassin "Ahmed Yassin"), along with his two bodyguards and nine bystanders. On 17 April, after several failed attempts by Hamas to commit suicide bombings and a successful one that killed an Israeli policeman, Yassin's successor, [Abdel Aziz al-Rantissi](/wiki/Abdel_Aziz_al-Rantissi "Abdel Aziz al-Rantissi"), was killed in an almost identical way, along with a bodyguard and his son Mohammed.

The fighting in Gaza Strip escalated severely in May 2004 after several failed attempts to attack [Israeli checkpoints](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces_checkpoint "Israel Defense Forces checkpoint") such as [Erez crossing](/wiki/Israeli_Gaza_Strip_barrier "Israeli Gaza Strip barrier") and [Karni crossing](/wiki/Karni_crossing "Karni crossing"). On 2 May, Palestinian militants attacked and [shot dead a pregnant woman and her four young daughters](/wiki/Murder_of_Tali_Hatuel_and_her_four_daughters "Murder of Tali Hatuel and her four daughters").[[122]](#cite_note-bbc-122)[[123]](#cite_note-smh20040503-123)[[124]](#cite_note-ii20040503-124) [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") classified it as a [crime against humanity](/wiki/Crime_against_humanity "Crime against humanity") and said it "reiterates its call on all Palestinian armed groups to put an immediate end to the deliberate targeting of Israeli civilians, in Israel and in the Occupied Territories".[[125]](#cite_note-ai-p-125) Additionally, on 11 and 12 May, Palestinian militants destroyed two IDF [M-113](/wiki/M113_Armored_Personnel_Carrier "M113 Armored Personnel Carrier") [APCs](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier"), killing 13 soldiers and mutilating their bodies. The IDF launched two raids to recover the bodies, killing 20–40 Palestinians and greatly damaging structures in the Zaitoun neighbourhood in Gaza and in south-west Rafah.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_40_Kilogram_Explosive_Found_At_Bottom_of_Tunnel.jpg/250px-Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_40_Kilogram_Explosive_Found_At_Bottom_of_Tunnel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_40_Kilogram_Explosive_Found_At_Bottom_of_Tunnel.jpg)

Israeli forces uncover a smuggling tunnel in Gaza, May 2004

Subsequently, on 18 May the IDF launched [Operation Rainbow](/wiki/Operation_Rainbow "Operation Rainbow") with a stated aim of striking the militant infrastructure of Rafah, destroying smuggling tunnels, and stopping a shipment of [SA-7](/wiki/Strela_2 "Strela 2") missiles and improved [anti-tank](/wiki/Anti-tank "Anti-tank") weapons. A total of 41 Palestinian militants and 12 civilians were killed in the operation, and about 45–56 Palestinian structures were demolished. Israeli tanks shelled hundreds of Palestinian protesters approaching their positions, killing 10. The protesters had disregarded Israeli warnings to turn back. This incident led to a worldwide outcry against the operation.

On 29 September, after a [Qassam rocket](/wiki/Qassam_rocket "Qassam rocket") hit the Israeli town of [Sderot](/wiki/Sderot "Sderot") and killed two Israeli children, the IDF launched [Operation Days of Penitence](/wiki/Operation_Days_of_Penitence "Operation Days of Penitence") in the north of the Gaza Strip. The operation's stated aim was to remove the threat of Qassam rockets from Sderot and kill the Hamas militants launching them. The operation ended on 16 October, after having caused widespread destruction and the deaths of over 100 Palestinians, at least 20 of whom were under the age of sixteen.[[126]](#cite_note-126) The IDF killed thirteen-year-old [Iman Darweesh Al Hams](/wiki/Iman_Darweesh_Al_Hams "Iman Darweesh Al Hams") as she strayed into a closed military area; the commander was accused of allegedly firing his automatic weapon at her dead body deliberately to verify the death. The act was investigated by the IDF, but the commander was cleared of all wrongdoing,[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128) and more recently, was fully vindicated when a Jerusalem district court found the claim to be libellous, ruled that NIS 300,000 be paid by the journalist and TV company responsible for the report, an additional NIS 80,000 to be paid in legal fees and required the journalist and television company to air a correction.[[129]](#cite_note-129) According to Palestinian medics, Israeli forces killed at least 62 militants and 42 other Palestinians believed to be civilians.[[130]](#cite_note-130) According to a count performed by *[Haaretz](/wiki/Haaretz "Haaretz")*, 87 militants and 42 civilians were killed. Palestinian refugee camps were heavily damaged by the Israeli assault. The IDF announced that at least 12 Qassam launchings had been thwarted and many militants hit during the operation.

On 21 October, the [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") killed [Adnan al-Ghoul](/wiki/Adnan_al-Ghoul "Adnan al-Ghoul"), a senior Hamas bomb maker and the inventor of the [Qassam rocket](/wiki/Qassam_rocket "Qassam rocket").

On 11 November, Yasser Arafat died in Paris.

Escalation in Gaza began amid the visit of [Mahmoud Abbas](/wiki/Mahmoud_Abbas "Mahmoud Abbas") to [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") in order to achieve a [Hudna](/wiki/Hudna "Hudna") between Palestinian factions and convince Hamas leadership to halt attacks against Israelis. Hamas vowed to continue the armed struggle, sending numerous [Qassam rockets](/wiki/Qassam_rocket "Qassam rocket") into open fields near [Nahal Oz](/wiki/Nahal_Oz "Nahal Oz"), and hitting a [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten") in [Kfar Darom](/wiki/Gush_Katif "Gush Katif") with an anti-tank missile.

On 9 December five Palestinians weapon smugglers were killed and two were arrested in the border between Rafah and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"). Later that day, [Jamal Abu Samhadana](/wiki/Jamal_Abu_Samhadana "Jamal Abu Samhadana") and two of his bodyguards were injured by a missile strike. In the first Israeli airstrike against militants in weeks, an unmanned Israeli drone plane launched one missile at Abu Samahdna's car as it travelled between Rafah and Khan Younis in the southern Gaza Strip. It was the fourth attempt on Samhadana's life by Israel. Samhadana is one of two leaders of the [Popular Resistance Committees](/wiki/Popular_Resistance_Committees "Popular Resistance Committees") and one of the main forces behind the smuggling tunnels. Samhadana is believed to be responsible for the [blast](/wiki/Improvised_explosive_device "Improvised explosive device") against an American diplomatic convoy in Gaza that killed three Americans.

On 10 December, in response to Hamas firing mortar rounds into the Neveh Dekalim settlement in the Gaza Strip and wounding four Israelis (including an 8-year-old boy), Israeli soldiers fired at the Khan Younis refugee camp (the origin of the mortars) killing a seven-year-old girl. An IDF source confirmed troops opened fire at Khan Younis, but said they aimed at Hamas mortar crews.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The largest attack since the death of Yasser Arafat claimed the lives of five Israeli soldiers on 12 December, wounding ten others. Approximately 1.5 tons of explosives were detonated in a tunnel under an Israeli military-controlled border crossing on the Egyptian border with Gaza near Rafah, collapsing several structures and damaging others. The explosion destroyed part of the outpost and killed three soldiers. Two Palestinian militants then penetrated the outpost and killed two other Israeli soldiers with gunfire. It is believed that Hamas and a new Fatah faction, the "Fatah Hawks", conducted the highly organised and coordinated attack. A spokesman, "Abu Majad", claimed responsibility for the attack in the name of the [Fatah Hawks](/wiki/Fatah_Hawks "Fatah Hawks") claiming it was in retaliation for "the assassination" of Yasser Arafat, charging he was poisoned by Israel.

### 2005

[Palestinian presidential elections](/wiki/2005_Palestinian_presidential_election "2005 Palestinian presidential election") were held on 9 January, and [Mahmoud Abbas](/wiki/Mahmoud_Abbas "Mahmoud Abbas") (Abu Mazen) was elected as the president of the PA. His platform was of a peaceful negotiation with Israel and non-violence to achieve Palestinian objectives. Although Abbas called on militants to halt attacks against Israel, he promised them protection from Israeli incursions and did not advocate disarmament by force.

Violence continued in the Gaza Strip, and [Ariel Sharon](/wiki/Ariel_Sharon "Ariel Sharon") froze all diplomatic and security contacts with the [Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority"). Spokesman Assaf Shariv declared that "Israel informed international leaders today that there will be no meetings with Abbas until he makes a real effort to stop the terror." The freezing of contacts came less than one week after Mahmoud Abbas was elected, and the day before his inauguration. Palestinian negotiator [Saeb Erekat](/wiki/Saeb_Erekat "Saeb Erekat"), confirming the news, declared "You cannot hold Mahmoud Abbas accountable when he hasn't even been inaugurated yet."[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Gaza_Strip_Access_Restrictions.pdf/page1-250px-Gaza_Strip_Access_Restrictions.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gaza_Strip_Access_Restrictions.pdf)

Gaza Strip, with borders and Israeli limited fishing zone

Following international pressure and Israeli threat of wide military operation in the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"), Abbas ordered [Palestinian police](/wiki/Preventive_Security_Force "Preventive Security Force") to deploy in the northern Gaza Strip to prevent [Qassam rocket](/wiki/Qassam_rocket "Qassam rocket") and mortar shelling over Israeli settlement. Although attacks on Israelis did not stop completely, they decreased sharply. On 8 February 2005, at the [Sharm el-Sheikh Summit of 2005](/wiki/Sharm_el-Sheikh_Summit_of_2005 "Sharm el-Sheikh Summit of 2005"), Sharon and Abbas declared a mutual [truce](/wiki/Truce "Truce") between Israel and the [Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority"). They shook hands at a four-way summit that also included [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") at [Sharm al-Sheikh](/wiki/Sharm_al-Sheikh "Sharm al-Sheikh"). However, Hamas and [Islamic Jihad](/wiki/Palestinian_Islamic_Jihad_Movement "Palestinian Islamic Jihad Movement") said the truce is not binding for their members. Israel has not withdrawn its demand to dismantle terrorist infrastructure before moving ahead in the [Road map for peace](/wiki/Road_map_for_peace "Road map for peace").[[133]](#cite_note-133)

Many warned that truce is fragile, and progress must be done slowly while observing that the truce and quiet are kept. On 9–10 February night, a barrage of 25–50 [Qassam rockets](/wiki/Qassam_rocket "Qassam rocket") and [mortar shells](/wiki/Mortar_(weapon) "Mortar (weapon)") hit [Neve Dekalim](/wiki/Neve_Dekalim "Neve Dekalim") settlement, and another barrage hit at noon. Hamas said it was in retaliation for an attack in which one Palestinian was killed near an Israeli settlement.[[134]](#cite_note-134) As a response to the mortar attack, Abbas ordered the Palestinian security forces to stop such attacks in the future. He also fired senior commanders in the Palestinian security apparatus. On 10 February, [Israeli security forces](/wiki/Israeli_security_forces "Israeli security forces") arrested Maharan Omar Shucat Abu Hamis, a Palestinian resident of [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus"), who was about to launch a bus [suicide attack](/wiki/Suicide_attack "Suicide attack") in the [French Hill](/wiki/French_Hill_(neighborhood) "French Hill (neighborhood)") in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem").

On 13 February 2005, Abbas entered into talks with the leaders of the Islamic Jihad and the Hamas, for them to rally behind him and respect the truce. Ismail Haniyah, a senior leader of the group Hamas said that "its position regarding calm will continue unchanged and Israel will bear responsibility for any new violation or aggression."

In the middle of June, Palestinian factions intensified bombardment over the city of [Sderot](/wiki/Sderot "Sderot") with improvised [Qassam rockets](/wiki/Qassam_rocket "Qassam rocket"). Palestinian attacks resulted in 2 Palestinians and 1 Chinese civilian killed by a Qassam, and 2 Israelis were killed. The wave of attacks lessened support for the [disengagement plan](/wiki/Israel%27s_unilateral_disengagement_plan "Israel's unilateral disengagement plan") among the Israeli public. Attacks on Israel by the [Islamic Jihad](/wiki/Palestinian_Islamic_Jihad_Movement "Palestinian Islamic Jihad Movement") and the [al-Aqsa Martyrs' Brigades](/wiki/Al-Aqsa_Martyrs%27_Brigades "Al-Aqsa Martyrs' Brigades") increased in July, and on 12 July, a [suicide bombing](/wiki/Suicide_attack "Suicide attack") hit the coastal city of [Netanya](/wiki/Netanya "Netanya"), killing 5 civilians. On 14 July, Hamas started to shell Israeli settlements inside and outside the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") with dozens of Qassam rockets, killing an Israeli woman. On 15 July, Israel resumed its "targeted killing" policy, killing 7 Hamas militants and bombing about 4 Hamas facilities. The continuation of shelling rockets over Israeli settlements, and street battles between Hamas militants and Palestinian policemen, threatened to shatter the truce agreed in the Sharm el-Sheikh Summit of 2005. The Israeli Defence Force also started to build up armored forces around the Gaza Strip in response to the shelling.

### End of the Second Intifada

The ending date of the Second Intifada is disputed, as there was no definite event that brought it to an end.[[135]](#cite_note-Schachter-135) The general view is that it ended in 2005, while some sources include events and statistics extending as late as 2007.[[136]](#cite_note-Plaw2016-136)

* Some commentators, such as Sever Plocker,[[137]](#cite_note-137) consider the intifada to have ended in late 2004. With the sickness and then death of Yasser Arafat in November 2004, the Palestinians lost their internationally recognised leader of the previous three decades, after which the intifada lost momentum and led to internal fighting between Palestinian factions (most notably the [Fatah–Hamas conflict](/wiki/Fatah%E2%80%93Hamas_conflict "Fatah–Hamas conflict")), as well as conflict within Fatah itself.
* Israel's unilateral disengagement from the Gaza Strip, announced in June 2004 and completed in August 2005, is also cited as signalling the end of the intifada, for instance by Ramzy Baroud.[[138]](#cite_note-138)
* Some consider 8 February 2005 to be the official end of the Second Intifada, although sporadic violence still continued outside PA control or condonation.[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140) On that day, Abbas and Sharon met at the [Sharm el-Sheikh Summit](/wiki/Sharm_el-Sheikh_Summit_of_2005 "Sharm el-Sheikh Summit of 2005"), where they vowed to end attacks on each other.[[141]](#cite_note-2005ceasefire-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142) In addition, Sharon agreed to release 900 [Palestinian prisoners](/wiki/Palestinian_prisoners_in_Israel "Palestinian prisoners in Israel") and withdraw from West Bank towns. Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad (PIJ) refused to be parties to the agreement, arguing the cease-fire was the position of the PA only.[[141]](#cite_note-2005ceasefire-141)[[143]](#cite_note-143) Five days later Abbas reached agreement with the two dissenting organizations to commit to the truce with the proviso that Israeli violation would be met with retaliation.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

Schachter addressed the difficulties in deciding when the Second Intifada ended. He reasoned that suicide bombing was the best criterion, being arguably the most important element of the violence involved, and that according to this criterion the intifada ended during 2005.[[135]](#cite_note-Schachter-135)

## Trigger for the uprising

The Second Intifada started on 28 September 2000, after [Ariel Sharon](/wiki/Ariel_Sharon "Ariel Sharon"), a Likud party candidate for Israeli Prime Minister, made a visit to the [Temple Mount](/wiki/Temple_Mount "Temple Mount"), also known as [Al-Haram Al-Sharif](/wiki/Temple_Mount#In_Islam "Temple Mount"), an area sacred to both Jews and Muslims, accompanied by over 1,000 security guards. He stated on that day, "the Temple Mount is in our hands and will remain in our hands. It is the holiest site in Judaism and it is the right of every Jew to visit the Temple Mount."[[145]](#cite_note-145)

This visit was seen by Palestinians as highly provocative; and Palestinian demonstrators, throwing stones at police, were dispersed by the Israeli Army, using tear gas and rubber bullets.[[146]](#cite_note-Mitchell_cause-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-bbctimeline-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149) A riot broke out among Palestinians at the site, resulting in clashes between Israeli forces and the protesting crowd.

Some believe the Intifada started the next day, on Friday, 29 September, a day of prayers, when an Israeli police and military presence was introduced and there were major clashes and deaths.[[150]](#cite_note-Mitchell-150)[[151]](#cite_note-Cypel-151)[[152]](#cite_note-Mittleman-152)

### The Mitchell Report

The Sharm el-Sheikh Fact-Finding Committee (an investigatory committee set up to look into the causes behind the breakdown in the peace process, chaired by [George J. Mitchell](/wiki/George_J._Mitchell "George J. Mitchell")) published its report in May 2001.[[153]](#cite_note-mitchell-153) In the [Mitchell Report](/wiki/Mitchell_Report_(Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict) "Mitchell Report (Arab–Israeli conflict)"), the government of Israel asserted that:

> The immediate catalyst for the violence was the breakdown of the Camp David negotiations on July 25, 2000, and the "widespread appreciation in the international community of Palestinian responsibility for the impasse". In this view, Palestinian violence was planned by the PA leadership, and was aimed at "provoking and incurring Palestinian casualties as a means of regaining the diplomatic initiative".

The Palestine Liberation Organization, according to the same report, denied that the Intifada was planned, and asserted that "Camp David represented nothing less than an attempt by Israel to extend the force it exercises on the ground to negotiations." The report also stated:

> From the perspective of the PLO, Israel responded to the disturbances with excessive and illegal use of deadly force against demonstrators; behavior which, in the PLO's view, reflected Israel's contempt for the lives and safety of Palestinians. For Palestinians, the widely seen images of Muhammad al-Durrah in Gaza on September 30, shot as he huddled behind his father, reinforced that perception.

The Mitchell report concluded:

> The Sharon visit did not cause the "Al-Aqsa Intifada". But it was poorly timed and the provocative effect should have been foreseen; indeed it was foreseen by those who urged that the visit be prohibited.

and also:

> We have no basis on which to conclude that there was a deliberate plan by the PA to initiate a campaign of violence at the first opportunity; or to conclude that there was a deliberate plan by the [Government of Israel] to respond with lethal force.

### Contributing factors

Palestinians have claimed that Sharon's visit was the beginning of the Second Intifada,[[148]](#cite_note-bbctimeline-148) while others have claimed that Yasser Arafat had pre-planned the uprising.[[154]](#cite_note-154)

Some, like [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"),[[155]](#cite_note-155) say that tensions were high due to failed negotiations at the [Camp David Summit](/wiki/2000_Camp_David_Summit "2000 Camp David Summit") in July 2000. They note that there were Israeli casualties as early as 27 September; this is the Israeli "conventional wisdom", according to Jeremy Pressman, and the view expressed by the [Israeli Foreign Ministry](/wiki/Israeli_Foreign_Ministry "Israeli Foreign Ministry").[[156]](#cite_note-pressman_backgroundsandcauses-156)[[157]](#cite_note-jpost2000sep29-157) Most mainstream media outlets have taken the view that the Sharon visit was the spark that triggered the rioting at the start of the Second Intifada.[[48]](#cite_note-bbc2000sept28-48)[[158]](#cite_note-nytimes2008sept30-158)[[159]](#cite_note-cnn2008sept28-159)[[160]](#cite_note-telegraph-160) In the first five days of rioting and clashes after the visit, Israeli police and security forces killed 47 Palestinians and wounded 1885,[[53]](#cite_note-Jerpro-53) while Palestinians killed 5 Israelis.[[161]](#cite_note-btselem-idf-OT-161)[[162]](#cite_note-btselem-civ-OT-162)

Palestinians view the Second Intifada as part of their ongoing struggle for national liberation and an end to Israeli occupation,[[163]](#cite_note-Schulz-163) whereas many [Israelis](/wiki/Israelis "Israelis") consider it to be a wave of Palestinian terrorism instigated and pre-planned by then Palestinian leader [Yasser Arafat](/wiki/Yasser_Arafat "Yasser Arafat").[[156]](#cite_note-pressman_backgroundsandcauses-156)
